Clinical Training
Diagnosis & Treatment Guide
How to conduct a diagnostic assessment — clinician reference

Draws on the DSM-5-TR, Black & Grant (DSM-5 Guidebook) and the SCID-5. A training aid, not a substitute for the manuals or clinical judgement. Use the per-disorder criteria and interview prompts above; use this for the interview skill around them.

The diagnostic process

A diagnosis emerges from assembling several sources, then testing it against alternatives: (1) chief complaint and history of the presenting problem; (2) psychiatric, medical, developmental, family and social history, plus current medications and substances; (3) the mental status examination; (4) collateral information and any measures; (5) a differential diagnosis, circling in on a provisional one; (6) severity, course, and specifiers. A provisional diagnosis is exactly that — revise it as information accrues; premature certainty is a common error.

Running the interview

Blend a clinical interview (following the person's story with warmth and open questions) with a structured one (systematically checking each criterion so nothing is missed, as in the SCID). Start open ('tell me what's been hardest'), then narrow to specific criteria. Anchor to real examples and to time — frequency, duration, and onset decide most criteria. Count a symptom only if it is clinically significant (causes distress or impairment), not merely present.

The mental status examination (MSE)

  • Appearance & behaviour — grooming, motor activity, eye contact, cooperation
  • Speech — rate, volume, quantity, fluency
  • Mood & affect — stated mood; observed range, reactivity, congruence
  • Thought process & content — coherence; delusions, obsessions, suicidal/homicidal ideation
  • Perception — hallucinations, dissociation
  • Cognition — orientation, attention, memory
  • Insight & judgement — understanding of the problem; decision-making

Differential diagnosis & ruling out medical causes

Before settling a diagnosis, exclude: another mental disorder that better accounts for the picture; a medical condition presenting with psychiatric symptoms (e.g. thyroid, neurological); substances or medication causing or mimicking symptoms; and an expectable reaction to a stressor or loss, which is not in itself a disorder.

Severity, specifiers & culture

After establishing a diagnosis, record severity (mild/moderate/severe), course specifiers (e.g. in partial remission, with anxious distress), and, where useful, a dimensional measure to track change. Symptoms and their meaning are shaped by culture: the Cultural Formulation Interview (CFI) helps you understand the person's own explanation of their problem and guards against mistaking a cultural norm for pathology.

Risk — every interview

Every diagnostic interview includes direct assessment of suicidal and homicidal ideation, self-harm, and safety. Diagnosis never takes precedence over immediate safety.
